The key differentiator is branching logic (J5): wi_skills includes conditional response paths driven by the skill's structured methodology, while wo_skills delivers only a linear call flow. Both outputs pass most criteria, but wi_skills demonstrates greater depth in objection-handling specificity (longer, more evidence-rich responses), more explicit ROI framing with specific metrics (3.2x ROI, 70% faster incident response embedded in pitch sections), and stronger integration of win story proof points within persuasion copy rather than as standalone references. The skill-driven output also shows more sophisticated proposal structure with phased discovery stages labeled with goals. wo_skills is a competent baseline document useful for reference, but lacks the branching interactivity and depth of embedded social proof that makes the wi_skills output immediately deployable for live rep use. Overall: wi_skills is clearly more effective.

items.criterion: The nurture program explicitly avoids or corrects at least 3 of the 5 failure patterns documented in the past outreach notes (e.g., avoids high-commitment CTAs too early, differentiates by company size, uses distinct subject lines, incorporates customer stories). Pass: the output references or reflects awareness of prior failure patterns with specific corrective choices. Fail: the program repeats the same structure as described in the failure notes (same-for-all emails, hard demo CTA in early touchpoints, no social proof).
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output has an explicit 'Why The Last Attempt Failed' section that calls out 5 specific failure patterns (demo too early, same message for all sizes, reused subject lines, high-commitment CTAs, ignored proof points) and states how each is corrected in the new program.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output explicitly addresses past failures in its Strategy section, listing 5 corrective actions (value-first, segmentation, low-commitment CTAs, proof points, triggered demo asks), and the Notes for Marketing Ops section reinforces these corrections.

items.criterion: The nurture sequence is designed with stage-appropriate content progression — early touchpoints offer value/education, mid-touchpoints build consideration with evidence, late touchpoints introduce low-friction conversion asks. Pass: at least 3 touchpoints are labeled or designed with stage logic and the CTA escalation is gradual (e.g., 'read this' → 'see a peer example' → 'get a personalized assessment'). Fail: all emails make the same demo request or have undifferentiated CTAs.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output structures a 5-email sequence with stage-labeled touchpoints (awareness/education early, case study/proof mid, personalized assessment/demo late) and an escalating CTA ladder from 'read' to 'see a peer example' to 'get a benchmark walkthrough'.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output defines a CTA ladder (low-commitment → mid-commitment → demo request), maps emails to awareness/consideration/decision stages, and uses engaged vs. unengaged branching with stage-appropriate content at each step.

items.criterion: The program provides differentiated content or messaging for at least 2 of the 3 company size sub-segments (100-200, 201-500, 501-800 employees), with substantive differences in pain framing or example relevance — not just a different company size number inserted into the same template. Pass: at least 2 variants exist with distinct opening hooks or value propositions that reflect different operational realities of those company sizes. Fail: a single template is used with a {{company_size}} variable as the only differentiation.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output defines three distinct segments (A: 100-200, B: 201-500, C: 501-800) with different pain framings ('no formal process yet' vs. 'broken spreadsheet workflows' vs. 'scale without enterprise complexity') and segment-specific offer angles, not just a size variable swap.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output defines three segments with distinct primary pain points and main angles per segment, with differentiated opening hooks (e.g., 'build a first scalable performance program' vs. 'create manager visibility and calibration discipline' vs. 'enterprise-grade visibility without enterprise complexity').

items.criterion: The program defines at least 2 behavioral triggers that branch the nurture path based on prospect engagement actions (e.g., 'if lead clicks content link, send follow-up X within 24 hours; if no open after 7 days, send re-engagement Y'). Pass: conditional logic with specific trigger events and corresponding actions is documented. Fail: the program is purely time-based with no behavioral branching.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output documents behavioral trigger logic including: if lead clicks content link → send follow-up within 24h; if no open after 7 days → send re-engagement variant; scoring thresholds triggering SDR routing. Conditional branching is explicitly documented.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output defines behavioral triggers: click or reply → add engagement score and continue to proof email; self-assessment completed or score ≥ 25 → route to SDR; no engagement by Day 21 → plain-text email; engaged with buying signals → send demo ask. Multiple conditional branches are documented.

items.criterion: The output includes at least one A/B test plan for the nurture sequence with a specific variable being tested (subject line angle, CTA type, email length), a hypothesis about which variant will perform better, and the metric used to determine the winner. Pass: test variable, hypothesis, and success metric are all present. Fail: subject line variants are listed without any testing framework or success criteria.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output includes an A/B testing section with specific variables being tested (subject line angle, CTA wording: 'Get the guide' vs. 'See the template' vs. 'Take the assessment'), a launch approach (10-15% pilot), and implies winner promotion to the remaining audience, though a formal hypothesis statement is somewhat implicit.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output includes an A/B testing section with specific test variables (subject line, CTA type, email length/format), explicit hypotheses per test (e.g., 'curiosity-framed subject line will outperform benchmark-reference line'), and success metrics (open rate, click rate, reply rate) with winner criteria.

items.criterion: Early touchpoint CTAs ask for micro-commitments (e.g., 'read a 2-minute case study', 'answer one question', 'download a template') rather than jumping to demo requests for a lead pool that has already ignored prior outreach. Pass: at least the first 2 touchpoints contain CTAs that require less than 15 minutes of prospect time and do not request a sales call. Fail: demo or sales call CTA appears in the first email.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output shows Email 1 CTA as 'read a 2-minute case study' and Email 2 CTA as 'download a template or take a self-assessment' — both are micro-commitments under 15 minutes with no demo request in the first two touchpoints.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output's first email uses a case study CTA and the second uses a self-assessment or template download — both are explicitly low-friction asks. The demo request is reserved for later touchpoints after engagement signals are detected.

items.criterion: The output specifies which metrics to track at different stages of the nurture sequence (e.g., open rate for awareness emails, click-through rate for consideration content, demo conversion rate for decision-stage emails) with enough specificity to diagnose where the funnel is breaking down. Pass: at least 3 stage-specific metrics are named with context on what a drop in that metric indicates. Fail: metrics section lists only top-level metrics like 'open rate and click rate' without stage differentiation.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output specifies stage-specific KPIs: open rate for awareness emails with diagnostic context, click-through rate for consideration content, and demo conversion/meeting rate for decision-stage emails, with notes on what a drop in each metric indicates about funnel breakdown.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output includes a measurement section with stage-differentiated metrics: open/click rates for awareness emails, content engagement and self-assessment completion for mid-funnel, demo conversion rate for decision stage, plus SDR conversion rate and re-engagement rate with diagnostic guidance.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 4 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality nurture programs that pass all 7 evaluation items. Both read the context files, addressed past outreach failures, created 3-segment differentiation, defined behavioral triggers, structured stage-appropriate CTA progressions, and included A/B testing and stage-specific metrics. The wi_skills output is slightly more concise and structured (explicit 'Why The Last Attempt Failed' section with a clean correction framework, cleaner segment table, more MAP-oriented workflow language). The wo_skills output is actually more detailed in some areas — particularly the A/B testing section, which includes explicit hypotheses and success metrics per test variable, making J5 stronger in wo_skills. The wo_skills output also provides more detailed measurement guidance. The difference is marginal: the skill helped the agent organize the output more systematically and use domain-specific MAP terminology (parent program, child streams, smart lists), but the baseline agent without the skill produced an equally comprehensive and actionable document by carefully reading the context files. Both outputs are production-quality. wi_skills has a slight structural/organizational edge; wo_skills has a slight edge in A/B testing rigor and measurement detail.
